name: Speed up datadog-static-analyzer scan on dd-source

description: Baseline run on main + autoresearch setup files. dd-source via dd-auth, 10/10 fingerprints match.

asi.hypothesis: establish baseline on main with full pipeline (dd-auth + analyzer + 10-repo deep fingerprint check)
asi.notes_for_next_iteration: First optimization to try: prior research's #6 (memchr fast-path in get_lines_to_ignore) — small but trivially safe and fully fingerprint-preserving. Then move to #4 (skip empty-bucket rules early) and #5 (reuse parse tree for is_test_file). Save the structurally-large change (#1 adaptive combined-query) for later when small wins are exhausted.
asi.noise_estimate: back-to-back baselines varied <2s on 250s base; ~1% noise floor

description: memchr fast-path in get_lines_to_ignore: bail when neither no-dd-sa nor datadog-disable appears anywhere in the file. (Prior research item #6.)

asi.hypothesis: vast majority of files have no DD disable marker; bail before the per-line whitespace-stripping + per-pattern .contains() loop
asi.code_change: 5-line fast-path at top of get_lines_to_ignore() using DISABLE_KEYWORDS.iter().any(|kw| code.contains(kw))
asi.why_it_works: 271k files * 2 string scans is much cheaper than 271k files * (line.chars().filter().collect()::<String>() per line + 1-6 .contains() per line). std::str::contains uses SIMD/memchr-optimized two-way search.
asi.next: try #4: skip empty-bucket rules early in the per-rule loop; or #5: reuse parse tree for is_test_file

description: Reuse parsed tree for is_test_file: analyze_with now returns (Vec<RuleResult>, Option<Arc<Tree>>); driver passes the tree to is_test_file instead of None. (Prior research item #5.)

asi.hypothesis: is_test_file re-parses the tree when called with None; reusing the analyze_with-parsed Arc<Tree> avoids 12k re-parses on dd-source
asi.noise_observation: two runs at 249.86 and 237.98 (~12s spread) reveal noise floor is wider than initial ~2s estimate — likely warm/cold page cache or dd-auth jitter. Median of 2 = 243.92.
asi.real_effect_size: negligible-to-tiny-positive on dd-source: most violation-bearing files (12k of 271k) hit has_test_like_path() first (returns early), so the reparse rarely happened anyway
asi.kept_for: API now exposes the parsed tree, enabling future tree-reuse work (e.g. for path_restrictions or downstream classifiers).
asi.next: literal pre-screen #2 — file-level: extract required substrings from #eq?/#any-of? predicates; before parsing/querying, skip language-rules whose literals aren't in the file. Big potential on dd-source where many rules touch zero files.

description: Per-rule + file-level literal pre-screen: extract required #eq?/#any-of? literals from each TS query at startup; skip parse+rule-loop entirely if no rule's literals are present in the file, and skip v8 dispatch per-rule otherwise. (Prior research item #2.)

asi.hypothesis: many of dd-source's ~63 active rules require specific literals (e.g. function names, package names) that are absent in most files. Conservative #eq?/#any-of? extraction lets us skip parse + v8 dispatch on those.
asi.implementation: TSQuery now carries a LiteralPreScreen built from the query source string (own parser — tree-sitter doesn't expose text predicates publicly). Conservative gates: pattern_count==1 only; bail on any '[' outside strings/comments (alternation); skip negative predicates; skip #match? (regex risk); skip capture-vs-capture #eq?.
asi.bug_caught_and_fixed: first version forgot that `[A B]@cap` reports pattern_count==1 even though it's alternation; predicates inside one branch aren't required by the other. Added has_alternation_bracket() bail. Found via dd-git-hooks fingerprint mismatch (go-security/command-injection lost 2 findings).
asi.tests_added: 12 LiteralPreScreen unit tests + 6 alternation-bracket tests; all 338 kernel tests pass.
asi.next: still room — try #1 adaptive combined-query for big-repo files; or extract more from #match? regexes with conservative gates (skip on |, (?i), [, ?, *, +).

description: Extend literal pre-screen to #match? regex predicates: extract longest contiguous literal run, with safety bails on top-level | alternation, (?...) group modifiers, and runs < 3 chars. Unlocks several big-budget rules (dc-url, v1-import-url, fabric.dog) that have only #match? predicates.

asi.hypothesis: many top-time rules (no-hardcoded-dc-url, v1-import-url, fabric.dog) only have #match? predicates with regexes that contain a long literal substring; extracting that substring conservatively unlocks them.
asi.implementation: extract_required_literal_from_regex(): walk regex, treat \X as literal X for X in regex meta-set, treat unescaped meta as run boundaries, pick longest run. Bail on top-level |, on (?...) group modifiers, and on runs <3 chars. Added 6 new tests; 350 kernel tests pass total.
asi.safety_check: fingerprint diff: zero changes vs baseline across all 10 reference repos. Rule output identical.
asi.next_potentially: multi-pattern handling (would unlock dd-trace-go-v2-migration/with-servicename, ~26s CPU, ~3s wall); JS-side const-array mining (would unlock no-hardcoded-cluster Go, ~85s CPU = ~10s wall biggest single rule); adaptive combined query (~2x potential per prior research).

description: Multi-pattern literal pre-screen: extract per-pattern (using start_byte_for_pattern / end_byte_for_pattern) and OR across patterns. Unlocks rules with multiple top-level patterns where every pattern is constrained (e.g. dd-trace-go-v2-migration/with-servicename: P1=#match? gopkg.in/...DataDog/dd-trace-go.v1/.* + P2=#eq? WithServiceName). Bails if any pattern is unconstrained.

asi.hypothesis: many top-time rules have 2+ top-level patterns, each requiring a literal. Single-pattern bail was leaving these on the table. Per-pattern extraction with cross-pattern OR captures them.
asi.implementation: LiteralPreScreen now stores Vec<Vec<Vec<String>>> = OR-over-patterns of AND-of-OR. extract_from_query() iterates query.pattern_count() and slices source via start/end_byte_for_pattern; each pattern's predicates extracted independently. Bail conditions: zero patterns; any pattern with [] alternation; any pattern with no extractable AND-groups (unconstrained → OR of patterns is unconstrained).
asi.tests_added: 3 multi-pattern tests using real Go grammar (both-constrained, one-unconstrained-bails, alternation-bracket-bails); 353 kernel tests pass total.
asi.rules_unlocked_examples: dd-trace-go-v2-migration/with-servicename (was 27s CPU on 100k Go files), with-withdogstatsaddress (26s)
asi.next: remaining big consumers: no-hardcoded-cluster (Go) at 87s CPU — needs JS-side mining but the rule does prefix matching (cluster.match(/^[a-z0-9]+/i)[0]), so naive array mining would miss findings. cluster-yml/datacenter-yml have [] alternation, also need JS mining. Combined query (#1) is the next big lever — estimated 2x potential for big workloads per prior research.
asi.noise: build_seconds 20.81 = full kernel rebuild after adding many lines

description: Smarter literal pre-screen: track [-depth and ?/*/+ quantifier suffixes precisely instead of bailing on any [-bracket. Predicates inside alternation or quantified groups are skipped; predicates at depth 0 in unquantified groups still extracted. Unlocks rules with localized [...] alternation but top-level required literals (e.g. python-flask/cookie-injection's #eq? set_cookie outside its inner alternation).

asi.hypothesis: many rules have a localized [...] alternation (e.g. for accepting either node form A or B) but a separate, unconditional predicate at depth 0 elsewhere in the pattern. The previous coarse 'any [' bail missed these. Tracking [-depth and quantifier suffixes lets us extract the always-required predicates while still skipping the conditional ones.
asi.implementation: two-pass walk of pattern source: pass 1 collects all (#xxx?) predicates with their open-paren stack; tracks open-paren positions whose closing ')' is followed by ?/*/+; pass 2 keeps only predicates at bracket-depth 0 whose enclosing opens are not in the conditional set. Removed coarse has_alternation_bracket bail.
asi.remaining_top_consumers: no-hardcoded-cluster (Go) 88s CPU — needs JS-side mining (risky due to JS prefix matching). Combined query (#1) is the next big lever.

description: Verification rerun (no code changes) — confirms 155-157s range stably reproduces. Current best holds.

asi.hypothesis: session noise floor confirmation: same binary, fresh run
asi.remarks: back-to-back wall delta 155.27 vs 156.65 = ~1s noise floor on 156s metric. confidence in 33-38% range improvements. Two runs at the same code: 155.27 / 156.65 → spread 1.4s.
asi.next_strategic_thinking: remaining big consumers are JS-heavy (no-hardcoded-cluster, datacenter) where TS-query-based screening can't help. Combined query (#1) would save ~3-5s wall for the shared interpreted_string_literal traversal but needs multi-hour surgery. JS-side const-array mining is unsafe for cluster rules due to JS prefix-extraction transforms (cluster.match(/^[a-z0-9]+/i)) — conservative gates per prior research would block this rule. Plateauing at -38%.

description: Pre-compute per-rule applicability mask once and reuse in the per-rule loop instead of re-evaluating each screen. Discarded: regressed wall by ~10s (164.93 vs 155.27).

asi.hypothesis: deduping redundant pre_screen.matches() between file-level any() and per-rule loop would save CPU; in practice it cost more.
asi.rollback_reason: +10s wall vs prev best. The previous .any() short-circuited on the FIRST passing rule — typical, since most files contain at least one common literal. The mask version eagerly evaluates ALL N rules' screens for EVERY file (no short-circuit), then allocates a Vec<bool>. Upfront work + alloc > saved redundant calls.
asi.next_action_hint: Don't replace short-circuit-friendly any() patterns with mask precomputation when the per-element cost is high. Keep the redundant per-rule check.
asi.fingerprints_unchanged: 10/10 match; correctness preserved — regression is purely perf.
asi.lesson: fast-path heuristics (any() short-circuiting, lazy evaluation) often beat eager precomputation when the early-exit case is common. Profile before refactoring the screen evaluation path.

description: JS-side const array literal mining (#3): for top-level `const NAME = [...]` arrays in rule JS code, mine each element as required (full + leading [a-zA-Z0-9_]+ prefix) and inject into the rule's pre-screen as a global OR-group. Conservative safety gates per prior research + extras. Unlocks no-hardcoded-cluster (Go) which was 89s CPU per scan.

asi.hypothesis: biggest single rule (no-hardcoded-cluster Go @ 89s CPU) has a top-level `const clusters = ['name.suffix.suffix.suffix', ...]` array. JS does both `match.includes(cluster)` (full) and `match.includes(cluster.match(/^[a-z0-9]+/i)[0])` (prefix). Mining BOTH the full element AND its leading-alphanumeric run safely covers both branches.
asi.implementation: new analysis/js_literal_miner.rs (~330 lines + 11 tests). Walks JS code tracking brace/paren depth, finds top-level const/let/var = [strings...] declarations. Safety gates: name no TYPE/KIND/NODE; at least one element non-alphanumeric (rules out AST type lists); name referenced elsewhere in JS (>1 occurrence); TS query has no [ outside strings/comments. For each element: emit both the full string and its leading [a-zA-Z0-9_]+ run when each is >=3 chars. LiteralPreScreen.add_global_required attaches them as a global AND-group on top of any TS-derived constraints.
asi.first_attempt_bug: v1 of the reference check (`code[after_decl..].contains(name)`) failed for arrays declared AFTER the visit() function (cluster rule's case). Switched to occurrence count > 1 across the whole file.
asi.what_else_was_unlocked: this also unlocks any other rule following the same pattern — cluster rules in other languages (-py, -starlark, -rust, -java) and various python-pandas/django rules with broad-capture queries + JS arrays.

description: Relax JS-mining's alternation gate: when TS query has [...] alternation BUT the rule's JS body references exactly one capture name (uniform handling across branches), allow JS array mining to proceed. Unlocks no-hardcoded-datacenter (Go) at 84s CPU and the YML cluster/datacenter rules. Verified again at 71.93s.

asi.hypothesis: the original `TS query has [` bail blocked too many rules from JS mining. Cluster/datacenter rules use TS alternation but ALL branches capture as a single `@literal` and the JS uniformly checks `query.captures.literal`. Counting distinct query.captures.<NAME> references in JS lets us prove the JS treats all branches the same way, which is sufficient for safety.
asi.implementation: added distinct_js_capture_count() that scans for query.captures.X / query.captures["X"] / capturesList variants and counts distinct names. mine_required_literals proceeds despite TS alternation iff exactly 1 distinct capture is referenced.
asi.verification_run: 73.17s, then 71.93s back-to-back — stable in 72-73s range, ~80x noise floor.
asi.tests_added: skips_when_ts_query_has_alternation_and_multiple_js_captures, allows_alternation_when_js_uses_single_capture; 12 miner tests + 350 base = 362 total kernel tests pass.
asi.rules_unlocked_estimate: no-hardcoded-datacenter (Go, 84s CPU saved), no-hardcoded-cluster-yml, no-hardcoded-datacenter-yml — all use single-capture JS uniformly across alternation branches.

description: Detect patterns whose @captures aren't referenced in JS body and exclude them from pre-screen (so an unused unconstrained pattern doesn't force the screen to bail). Discarded: neutral on dd-source (74.26 / 72.68 vs prev best 71.93, within noise) — added code complexity without measurable gain.

asi.hypothesis: rules like python-flask/command-injection have an unused auxiliary pattern that prevents pre-screen extraction. Detecting JS-unused patterns and dropping them from screen-building should let pattern 1's `#eq? "subprocess"` constrain the screen.
asi.rollback_reason: neutral on dd-source. Two runs at 74.26/72.68s vs prev best 71.93s — within ~3s noise floor. Likely "subprocess" appears in most Python files in dd-source, so the screen barely filters anything for python-flask/command-injection.
asi.next_action_hint: diminishing returns: top-time consumers now have screens that don't filter much because the required literals are common. Further gains likely need either combined-query (shared traversal across rules) or per-file overhead reductions (file walking, parse cost), not more screening cleverness.
asi.code_lessons: the architecture (rebuild_pre_screen_filtered + js_capture_references + pattern_capture_names) is clean and could be reused by future ideas; just doesn't help THIS workload.

description: Verification rerun (no code changes). Range 71.93–74.26 confirms stable plateau at ~72s. Logging as discard since no improvement vs prev best.

asi.hypothesis: session noise floor at the new plateau
asi.rollback_reason: no code change; primary metric did not improve. Logging as observation only.
asi.next_action_hint: remaining wall is dominated by per-file overhead (parsing trees, file walking, classification) and the small long tail of remaining rules. Real further gains likely need either: (a) parallelism scaling (current default caps at min(num_cpus, 8), using ~6 worker threads of 16 available; lifting that cap could nearly halve analyzer Duration); (b) combined Tree-sitter query for shared traversal (~5s wall), (c) language-level parallelism (current code serializes across languages — Go finishes before Python starts). All of these are structural changes outside the conservative spirit of this session.
